content.problemStatement:
An array BBB of length MMM is said to be smoothly increasing if it satisfies either of the following conditions:


M=1M = 1M=1, or
B1<B2<⋯<BMB_1 \lt B_2 \lt \cdots \lt B_MB1​<B2​<⋯<BM​, and the difference array [B2−B1,B3−B2,…,BM−BM−1][B_2 - B_1, B_3 - B_2, \ldots, B_M - B_{M-1}][B2​−B1​,B3​−B2​,…,BM​−BM−1​] is smoothly increasing.
That is, an array is smoothly increasing if it either has length 111, or it is strictly increasing and its difference array is smoothly increasing.

Note that the definition is recursive.

For example, [1],[5,9],[10,100,1000][1], [5, 9], [10, 100, 1000][1],[5,9],[10,100,1000] are smoothly increasing arrays, while [5,3][5, 3][5,3] and [10,20,30][10, 20, 30][10,20,30] are not.

You are given an array AAA of length NNN. For each index iii (1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N), answer the following question independently:


Delete the element AiA_iAi​ from AAA, without changing the order of the remaining elements. Is the resulting array smoothly increasing?

Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the length of the array.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output a binary string of length NNN on a new line.

The iii-th character of this binary string should be 111 if deleting AiA_iAi​ results in a smoothly increasing array, and 000 otherwise.
constraints: The sum of NNN across all tests won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Removing any element will result in a smoothly increasing array. For example, deleting A2=10A_2 = 10A2​=10 gives [1,100,1000][1, 100, 1000][1,100,1000], which behaves as follows:

[1,100,1000][1, 100, 1000][1,100,1000] is strictly increasing.
Its difference array is [99,900][99, 900][99,900] which is strictly increasing.
The difference array of this is [801][801][801] which is a singleton and so smoothly increasing.
So, the initial array is smoothly increasing.

Test case 222: Deleting A3=2A_3 = 2A3​=2 results in a smoothly increasing array. Deleting any other element will not even give a sorted array.
Test case 333: Deleting A4=20A_4 = 20A4​=20 will not result in a smoothly increasing array, because:

Initially, the array is [11,12,13,50][11, 12, 13, 50][11,12,13,50] which is strictly increasing.
The difference array is [1,1,37][1, 1, 37][1,1,37] which is not strictly increasing; so the initial array is not smoothly increasing.

Similarly, deleting A5=50A_5 = 50A5​=50 will not result in a smoothly increasing array. However, deleting any of the first three elements will work.
